claims.claim_text: Zillow announced in late 2021 that it would wind down Zillow Offers, with losses on the order of $500M+ and a workforce reduction of about 25%.
claims.span_hint: section: The $500 Million Black Box
claims.rationale: The company IR release is the primary source, but the Zillow investor host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Zillow said the shutdown was driven by the difficulty of forecasting home prices with acceptable earnings and balance-sheet volatility.
claims.span_hint: section: The $500 Million Black Box
claims.rationale: This wording is directly grounded in Rich Barton's statement, but the source host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes Zillow's failure to engineers missing distribution shifts and exploding error gradients in internal risk metrics.
claims.span_hint: section: The $500 Million Black Box
claims.anchor_text: The engineers treating their predictive engines as infallible black boxes failed to diagnose the underlying distribution shifts and exploding error gradients in their financial risk metrics.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The episode is a reminder that model failures often become costly when teams over-trust predictions and fail to investigate changing data conditions.
claims.rationale: These internal causal details are not supported by a cited postmortem or primary source in the module.

claims.claim_text: AlexNet's ILSVRC-2012 result was 15.3% top-5 error versus 26.2% for the second-best entry, by a team of Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey Hinton.
claims.span_hint: section: The Day a Machine Learned to See
claims.rationale: The CACM version of the AlexNet paper directly states the 15.3% versus 26.2% comparison and names the authors.

claims.claim_text: The narrative places Hinton in Toronto on December 3, 2012 at 4:47 PM when the ImageNet results arrived.
claims.span_hint: section: The Day a Machine Learned to See
claims.suggested_rewrite: **In 2012, the ImageNet results marked a dramatic turning point for deep learning.**
claims.rationale: The precise time-and-place framing is anecdotal and needs a traceable interview or primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module says reviewers initially assumed AlexNet's jump was a mistake.
claims.span_hint: section: The Day a Machine Learned to See
claims.anchor_text: A single neural network had reduced the error rate by more than 10 percentage points—a jump so large that reviewers initially assumed it was a mistake.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A single neural network had reduced the error rate by more than 10 percentage points, making the result feel unusually large by the standards of the time.
claims.rationale: The benchmark gap is citable, but the reviewer reaction is not sourced here.

claims.claim_text: The Geoffrey Hinton quote beginning 'I remember thinking: this changes everything' is presented as a real 2017 quotation.
claims.span_hint: section: The Day a Machine Learned to See
claims.anchor_text: > "I remember thinking: this changes everything. The ideas we'd been developing for decades—they actually work. We just needed scale."
claims.suggested_rewrite: > The AlexNet result was widely seen as a turning point for deep learning, but this exact quotation needs a verifiable interview source.
claims.rationale: Exact quotations require a traceable interview, transcript, or published source.

claims.claim_text: Google acquired Hinton's startup for $44 million, and within a year neural networks became the dominant AI paradigm and later powered systems like ChatGPT.
claims.span_hint: section: The Day a Machine Learned to See
claims.anchor_text: Within months, Google acquired Hinton's startup for $44 million. Within a year, neural networks would be the dominant paradigm in AI. Within a decade, they would power everything from voice assistants to self-driving cars to ChatGPT.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Soon after the AlexNet breakthrough, major technology companies accelerated their investment in deep learning, and neural networks became central to many high-profile AI systems.
claims.rationale: This combines an unsourced acquisition figure with broad historical claims that are too compressed to verify as written.

claims.claim_text: The core backpropagation algorithm used in modern deep learning was published in a landmark 1986 paper.
claims.span_hint: section: The Day a Machine Learned to See
claims.rationale: The canonical primary source is the 1986 Nature paper, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: As of March 23, 2026, PyTorch 2.11.0 was the latest stable release.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern Framework: PyTorch 2.11.0 Context
claims.rationale: The official GitHub releases page marks v2.11.0 as the latest release on March 23, 2026.

claims.claim_text: The PyTorch documentation version selector specifically advertised v2.9.0 as the stable release.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern Framework: PyTorch 2.11.0 Context
claims.anchor_text: You must navigate official documentation with technical skepticism: the PyTorch documentation version selector sometimes inconsistently advertises v2.9.0 as the stable release.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You should verify version-sensitive installation guidance against authoritative release metadata such as official release notes.
claims.rationale: This is a transient website-state claim with a hardcoded version that is not stable enough to preserve.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch 2.11.0 supports Python 3.10 through 3.14, with 3.14t marked experimental.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern Framework: PyTorch 2.11.0 Context
claims.rationale: The most direct current evidence is PyPI wheel metadata, but pypi.org is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch 2.11.0 is officially supported on Linux with glibc>=2.17, macOS 10.15+, and Windows 7/Server 2008 R2+.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern Framework: PyTorch 2.11.0 Context
claims.anchor_text: It is officially supported on Linux (glibc>=2.17), macOS 10.15+, and Windows 7/Server 2008 R2+.
claims.suggested_rewrite: PyTorch publishes platform-specific support and wheel constraints for Linux, macOS, and Windows, so you should verify them against the current release metadata before installing.
claims.rationale: The exact platform floor claims are brittle and do not cleanly match the currently visible package metadata.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch 2.11 stable binary support covered CUDA 12.6 and 12.8, with CUDA 13.0 treated as experimental.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern Framework: PyTorch 2.11.0 Context
claims.anchor_text: The stable binary support covers CUDA 12.6, CUDA 12.8, and experimental CUDA 13.0 architectures.
claims.suggested_rewrite: PyTorch 2.11 ships multiple CUDA wheel variants, and you should choose the wheel that matches your driver and GPU architecture.
claims.rationale: The stable-versus-experimental framing is not accurate enough for the 2.11 release packaging changes.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch 2.11 removed Volta support from CUDA 12.8/12.9 prebuilt binaries and moved default PyPI wheels to CUDA 13.0.
claims.span_hint: section: The Modern Framework: PyTorch 2.11.0 Context
claims.rationale: The 2.11 release notes explicitly describe the Volta removal and the PyPI CUDA 13.0 default.

claims.claim_text: The first mathematical model of a neuron was proposed in 1943 by McCulloch and Pitts in 'A Logical Calculus of the Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity.'
claims.span_hint: section: The Tumultuous History of Neural Networks
claims.rationale: The original paper is the right primary source, but Springer is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module says a 1958 New York Times article predicted Rosenblatt's perceptron would 'walk, talk, see, write, reproduce itself and be conscious of its existence.'
claims.span_hint: section: The Tumultuous History of Neural Networks
claims.anchor_text: In 1958, Frank Rosenblatt built the Mark I Perceptron, generating massive but premature hype (with The New York Times claiming it would eventually "walk, talk, see, write, reproduce itself and be conscious of its existence").
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 1958, Frank Rosenblatt built the Mark I Perceptron, and press coverage helped fuel early hype around neural networks.
claims.rationale: The exact newspaper quotation needs a primary newspaper source, not a paraphrased retelling.

claims.claim_text: Perceptrons (1969) established important limitations of single-layer perceptrons, including the XOR-style limitation cited here.
claims.span_hint: section: The Tumultuous History of Neural Networks
claims.rationale: The canonical source is the book itself, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The quote that Hinton was 'pretty much the only person in the world who still believed in neural networks' is presented as a real quotation.
claims.span_hint: section: The Tumultuous History of Neural Networks
claims.anchor_text: during which Hinton later noted he "was pretty much the only person in the world who still believed in neural networks."
claims.suggested_rewrite: during which only a relatively small group of researchers continued investing heavily in neural-network ideas.
claims.rationale: Exact quotations need a verifiable interview or publication.

claims.claim_text: MNIST was created by Yann LeCun in 1998 from NIST handwriting data, mixing samples from Census Bureau employees and high school students and centering anti-aliased digits in 28x28 images.
claims.span_hint: section: Practical Application: MNIST Digit Recognition
claims.rationale: The canonical dataset description is on Yann LeCun's MNIST page, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Modern methods can exceed 99.5% accuracy on MNIST.
claims.span_hint: section: It's Actually Too Easy Now
claims.rationale: The CACM AlexNet article states that the then-current best MNIST error was below 0.3%, which implies accuracy above 99.5%.

claims.claim_text: Humans achieve about 97.5% accuracy on MNIST.
claims.span_hint: section: Human Performance
claims.anchor_text: Humans achieve about 97.5% accuracy on MNIST (yes, some digits are ambiguous!).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Humans also make mistakes on a small fraction of MNIST digits because some examples are genuinely ambiguous.
claims.rationale: This precise percentage needs a direct primary source and should not be left as an unattributed number.

claims.claim_text: A simple neural net with no hidden layers gets about 92% accuracy on MNIST.
claims.span_hint: section: Expected Accuracy Baselines
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Simple neural net (no hidden layers) | strong baseline performance, depending on setup |
claims.rationale: This is a setup-sensitive benchmark figure presented without a source.

claims.claim_text: A two-layer neural net gets about 97% accuracy on MNIST.
claims.span_hint: section: Expected Accuracy Baselines
claims.suggested_rewrite: | 2-layer neural net | typically outperforms a no-hidden-layer baseline, depending on setup |
claims.rationale: This benchmark number depends heavily on architecture and training details and is unsourced here.

claims.claim_text: State-of-the-art CNNs get about 99.8% accuracy on MNIST.
claims.span_hint: section: Expected Accuracy Baselines
claims.suggested_rewrite: | CNN (state of art) | very high accuracy on MNIST |
claims.rationale: This exact leaderboard-style number needs a direct benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: He initialization is named after Kaiming He and was introduced for rectifier-based deep networks.
claims.span_hint: section: Weight Initialization
claims.rationale: The arXiv paper 'Delving Deep into Rectifiers' is the primary source for the initialization scheme.

claims.claim_text: Official PyTorch Conda publishing was dropped starting with the 2.6 release.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / Conda Support Dropped
claims.rationale: The 2.6 release notes explicitly state that official Conda publishing stops with that release.

claims.claim_text: The ecosystem-versioning sentence says torchvision was 0.26, torchaudio was 2.10.0, and torchtext was 0.18.0 while PyTorch itself was 2.11.0.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / Ecosystem Fragmentation
claims.anchor_text: While PyTorch itself sits at 2.11.0, the Torchvision library (vital for image manipulation) tracks stable release v0.26. Torchaudio is currently at v2.10.0, and Torchtext sits at v0.18.0.
claims.suggested_rewrite: PyTorch domain libraries do not always share the same version numbering, and some libraries such as torchtext follow a separate maintenance path.
claims.rationale: This mixes one correct and at least one stale library-version claim, so the safest fix is to remove the brittle exact versions.

claims.claim_text: Cybenko's 1989 Universal Approximation Theorem established that a single hidden layer can approximate any continuous function under the theorem's conditions.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / Universal Function Approximators
claims.rationale: The canonical paper is on Springer, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A function that needs 2^n neurons in a shallow network might need only n layers in a deep network.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / So why go deep?
claims.anchor_text: - A function that needs 2^n neurons in a shallow network might need only n layers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Some functions can be represented far more compactly by deep networks than by shallow ones.
claims.rationale: This exact asymptotic claim is too specific to leave uncited and too compressed to support honestly as written.

claims.claim_text: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis sentence names the second author incorrectly and overstates the result as matching the exact accuracy of the full model.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis
claims.anchor_text: A revolutionary 2019 MIT paper by Jonathan Frankle and Michael Carlin demonstrated that massive deep neural networks contain tiny, sparsely connected subnetworks (the "winning tickets") that can independently match the exact accuracy of the giant model.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A 2019 paper by Jonathan Frankle and Michael Carbin argued that large neural networks can contain much smaller subnetworks that train effectively on their own.
claims.rationale: The module has a wrong author name and an over-precise performance claim, so it needs a corrective rewrite rather than a citation alone.

claims.claim_text: The module dates key vanishing-gradient remedies as ReLU (2010), Batch Normalization (2015), and ResNet skip connections (2015).
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / The Vanishing Gradient Problem
claims.rationale: This timeline is real and verifiable, but the most direct source for the ReLU date is off-allowlist.

further_reading.title: PyTorch 2.11.0 Release Notes
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the cleanest allowlisted source for the module's version-specific PyTorch packaging and CUDA claims.

further_reading.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the classic AlexNet paper and directly supports several of the historical and benchmark claims in the module.

further_reading.title: Delving Deep into Rectifiers
further_reading.why_relevant: This paper is directly relevant to the module's discussion of ReLU networks and He initialization.

notes: Allowlist gaps force needs_allowlist_expansion for Zillow investor relations, PyPI, yann.lecun.org, Nature, Springer, MIT Press, and historical quote sources. Several narrative flourishes in the AlexNet and PyTorch sections are better handled as deterministic rewrites than by forcing weak citations.
